original_abstract: This article examines the changing landscape of global student mobility and the shifting market position of the United States as a premier destination for international higher education. While the U.S. remains the leading host for international students, recent data reveals a decline in its global market share alongside significantly higher growth rates in competitor nations such as France, Germany, Japan, and Australia. The analysis highlights how various countries are employing proactive national strategies to attract talent, including the easing of immigration and work restrictions, the expansion of English-medium instruction in non-English speaking countries, and the development of regional education hubs. Additionally, the paper discusses the impact of domestic capacity building in traditional source countries like China and India, which increasingly aim to retain students within their own borders. The findings suggest that the U.S. can no longer take its dominance for granted as international competition intensifies and global student flows become more diversified.
humanized_abstract: This paper examines the dynamics of global student mobility and the decline of the US in the international student market. While the US is still the number one country in terms of the total number of international students hosted, its global market share is decreasing while that of its competitors such as France, Germany, Japan and Australia is increasing at a much faster rate. The paper describes how the major countries are undertaking policy measures to try and attract students including easing immigration and work permit restrictions, increasing the amount of instruction in the English language in non-English speaking countries and by trying to position regions such as Singapore, Malaysia and Panama as major international education hubs. The paper also examines capacity building within the traditional sending countries such as China and India. These countries have a growing capacity to compete with other countries for international students because they are increasingly able to provide a high quality education to students who choose not to go abroad. Our findings suggest that the US is no longer the automatic leader in terms of international student recruitment due to a rise in global competition and the increased diversity of the international student mobility market.
